coredata.dc:title: Decoration of carbon dots over hydrogen peroxide treated graphitic carbon nitride: Exceptional photocatalytic performance in removal of different contaminants under visible light
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry
coredata.dc:creator: Asadzadeh-Khaneghah, Soheila
Habibi-Yangjeh, Aziz
Nakata, Kazuya
coredata.dc:description:
The development of novel photocatalysts with considerable activity for completely removal of different pollutants from the environment is a dominating goal of modern chemistry. In this study, carbon dots (CDs) were adhered to the graphitic carbon nitride activated by hydrogen peroxide (ag-C3N4) to fabricate photocatalysts with exceptional ability upon visible-light illumination. Interestingly, the BET surface area, visible-light absorption characteristics, and electron-hole separation yield of the pristine g-C3N4 were improved after activation with H2O2 and decoration of CDs. The binary nanocomposite was used for degradation of MB, RhB, fuchsine, and phenol and photoreduction of Cr(VI) under visible light. The nanocomposite exhibited excellent photocatalytic performance with 100% removal of RhB in 60 min, which is almost 34.7 folds as premier as the pristine g-C3N4. Reactive species scavenging measurements displayed that
O2 −,
OH, and h+ had significant roles for photodegradation of RhB. The possible mechanism was proposed regarding how activation with H2O2 and decoration of CDs improved the photocatalytic performance of g-C3N4. Also, stability of the composite was characterized through cyclic photocatalytic tests.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphitic carbon nitride
Hydrogen peroxide treated g-C3N4
g-C3N4/CDs
Visible-light-active photocatalyst

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry
xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst primabst ref specialabst

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration for preparation of the ag-C3N4/Carbon Dots nanocomposites.
XRD patterns for the g-C3N4, ag-C3N4-1M, ag-C3N4-2M, ag-C3N4-3M, ag-C3N4-4M, and ag-C3N4-3M/CDs (0.5 mL) photocatalysts.
(a) EDX spectra for the g-C3N4, ag-C3N4-3M, and ag-C3N4-3M/CDs (0.5 mL) samples. (b–e) EDX mapping of the ag-C3N4-3M/CDs (0.5 mL) nanocomposite.
(a) SEM, (b) TEM, (c) HRTEM images of the ag-C3N4-3M/CDs (0.5 mL) nanocomposite.
XPS spectra for the ag-C3N43 M/CDs (0.5 mL) nanocomposite: (a) Survey scan, and high-resolution spectra for: (b) C 1 s, (c) N 1 s, and (d) O 1 s.
(a) FT-IR spectra for the g-C3N4, ag-C3N4-3M, and ag-C3N4-3M/CDs (0.5 mL) samples. (b) UV-vis DRS for the g-C3N4, CDs, ag-C3N4-3M, and different nanocomposites of ag-C3N4-3M/CDs.
The upconverted PL spectra of the as-synthesized CDs at different excitation wavelengths.
TG analysis of the g-C3N4, ag-C3N4-3M, and different composites of ag-C3N4-3M/CDs (0.5 mL) samples.
Photodegradation of RhB over the (a) g-C3N4 and ag-C3N4-(1–4 M) samples, (b) different nanocomposites of ag-C3N4-3M/CDs in comparison with the g-C3N4, g-C3N4/CDs (0.5 mL), and ag-C3N4-3M samples.
UV–vis spectra for degradation of RhB under visible light over the (a) g-C3N4, (b) ag-C3N4-3M, and (c) ag-C3N4-3M/CDs (0.5 mL) composite.

(a) The degradation rate constants of RhB over the g-C3N4, ag-C3N4-(1–4 M), g-C3N4/CDs (0.5 mL), and different nanocomposites of ag-C3N4-3M/CDs. (b) PL spectra for the g-C3N4, ag-C3N4-2M, ag-C3N4-3M, ag-C3N4-4M, and ag-C3N4-3M/CDs (0.5 mL). (c) Nitrogen adsorption-desorption data for the g-C3N4, ag-C3N4-3M, and ag-C3N4-3M/CDs (0.5 mL) samples.
A plausible diagram for separation of the charges in the ag-C3N4-3M/CDs nanocomposites.
The degradation rate constants of RhB over the ag-C3N4-3M/CDs (0.5 mL) nanocomposite in various conditions.
Cycling runs for removal of RhB by the ag-C3N4-3M/CDs (0.5 mL) nanocomposite.
The degradation rate constants of RhB, MB, fuchsine, phenol, and reduction of Cr (VI) over the g-C3N4, ag-C3N4-3M, and ag-C3N4-3M/CDs (0.5 mL) samples.
The TOC contents for aqueous solutions of RhB, MB, fuchsine, and phenol during the degradation reaction over the ag-C3N4-3M/CDs (0.5 mL) nanocomposite.
Weight percentages of different elements in the ag-C3N4-(1–4 M) samples and different nanocomposites of the ag-C3N4-3M/CDs.
The textural properties of g-C3N4, ag-C3N4-3M, and ag-C3N4-3M/CDs (0.5 mL) samples.

head.ce:title: Decoration of carbon dots over hydrogen peroxide treated graphitic carbon nitride: Exceptional photocatalytic performance in removal of different contaminants under visible light

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science, University of Mohaghegh Ardabili, P.O. Box 179, Ardabil, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Photocatalysis International Research Center, Tokyo University of Science, 2641 Yamazaki, Noda-shi, Chiba, 278-8510, Japan

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
The development of novel photocatalysts with considerable activity for completely removal of different pollutants from the environment is a dominating goal of modern chemistry. In this study, carbon dots (CDs) were adhered to the graphitic carbon nitride activated by hydrogen peroxide (ag-C3N4) to fabricate photocatalysts with exceptional ability upon visible-light illumination. Interestingly, the BET surface area, visible-light absorption characteristics, and electron-hole separation yield of the pristine g-C3N4 were improved after activation with H2O2 and decoration of CDs. The binary nanocomposite was used for degradation of MB, RhB, fuchsine, and phenol and photoreduction of Cr(VI) under visible light. The nanocomposite exhibited excellent photocatalytic performance with 100% removal of RhB in 60 min, which is almost 34.7 folds as premier as the pristine g-C3N4. Reactive species scavenging measurements displayed that
O2 −,
OH, and h+ had significant roles for photodegradation of RhB. The possible mechanism was proposed regarding how activation with H2O2 and decoration of CDs improved the photocatalytic performance of g-C3N4. Also, stability of the composite was characterized through cyclic photocatalytic tests.

ce:section.ce:para: Although 70% of the earth has surrounded with water, almost 2.5% is proper for drinking, agriculture, and industrial consumptions. Hence, due to water scarcity, implementation of water reuse has gained much more attention worldwide [1–4]. Recently, it has seen an unprecedentedly large number of researches on the wastewater treatment through advanced oxidation processes [5–9]. Among these processes, semiconductor-based photocatalysis has been mentioned as a green, inexpensive, efficient, and promising method to convert renewable solar energy for solving the environmental crisis through degradation of hazardous contaminants into low-toxic molecules such as H2O and CO2 [10–15].

Since 2009, researchers have been enormously attracted in new categories of carbon-based photocatalysts, like graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4)-based materials with great photocatalytic performance under visible light [16–19]. This organic semiconductor has emerged as an attractive photocatalyst owing to its appealing characteristics of good visible-light response, high chemical and thermal stability, availability of simple synthesis methods, low cost, and nontoxicity [20–23]. However motivated by its limited absorption in visible area, high recombination rate of the photoinduced charges, and low surface area, many researchers have endeavored to modify this semiconductor to ameliorate its photocatalytic ability and its disadvantages [24–32].

In most conditions, a photocatalyst is generally needed to generate reactive oxidizing species. Furthermore, to prevent destruction of the g-C3N4 structure, oxidants with lower redox potentials should be used [33–35]. As we know, H2O2 is a green oxidant commonly used in water purification [36,37]. Hence, H2O2 treatment method was applied to modify characteristics of g-C3N4 with the purpose of improving its photocatalytic ability [36–40]. Furthermore, to broader visible-light absorption characteristics and much more facilitating separation efficiency of the photogenerated charges, heterojunction was formed with carbon dots (CDs) due to its unique electron mediating feature [41,42]. The results displayed that the amount of H2O2 applied in the treatment process and CDs content of the binary nanocomposites had an important influence on the photoactivity. In these regards, the as-synthesized binary photocatalysts formed by activating with 3 M of H2O2 and combined with the 0.5 mL of CDs solution displayed remarkable activity in degradations of several organic contaminants including MB, RhB, fuchsine, and phenol under visible light. Additionally, photoreduction of Cr(VI), as an inorganic dye, was done and superior photoability was seen for the binary nanocomposite. The trapping experiments displayed that
O2 −,
OH, and h+ had major role in the decomposition of RhB. Finally, a probable photocatalytic mechanism was suggested for the photocatalytic reactions.

The g-C3N4 powder was prepared using melamine (Loba Chemie) in two steps. Briefly, 6 g melamine was heated to 500 °C for 2 h and then heated to 520 °C for 2 h with a heating rate of 10 °C min−1 [43]. The yellow g-C3N4 powder was treated with H2O2 (Loba Chemie, wt. 30%). Briefly, 100 mL of aqueous H2O2 solution (3 M) containing 0.4 g of the bulk g-C3N4 was stirred in a beaker for 60 min. Thereafter, the suspension was washed many times with water followed washing with ethanol and the product was obtained with drying at 60 °C. Finally, the dark yellow-to-orange powder was obtained and it was labelled as ag-C3N4-3M. The obtained ag-C3N4-xM samples with different concentrations of H2O2 varying from 1 to 4 M were denoted as ag-C3N4-1M, ag-C3N4-2M, ag-C3N4-3M, and ag-C3N4-4M, respectively.

For preparation of CDs, 3 g citric acid (Merck, 99%) and 3 g urea (Merck, 99%) were added to 10 mL water, resulting in a transparent solution. The solution was irradiated for 5 min in a domestic 750 W microwave oven. During this process, the solution is changed from a colorless liquid to a dark-brown solid. The resultant solid was heated at 60 °C for 1 h in an oven to remove the volatile molecules. Afterwards, the resultant powder of CDs was dissolved in 180 mL of water. Then, the suspension was centrifuged (7500 rmin−1, 10 min) to remove agglomerated particles of the CDs solution. The resulting colored (brown) aqueous solution remained stable for a long time [44].

Finally, for preparation of the ag-C3N4-3M/CDs (0.5 mL) photocatalyst, where 0.5 mL is volume of the used CDs, primarily 0.4 g of the ag-C3N4-3M and 0.5 mL of CDs were sonicated in 100 mL water within 5 min. Then, the resultant suspension oven dried at 80 °C overnight (Scheme 1 ).
The detailed characterization instruments are in the supporting materials.

Photocatalytic activities were estimated by photocatalytic decomposition of 250 mL of RhB and MB (1 × 10−5 M), fuchsine (0.77 × 10–5 M), phenol (5 × 10−5 M) and photoreduction of Cr(VI) (100 mg/L). In detail, 0.1 g of the desired photocatalyst was sonicated in 5 mL of water for 6 min. Before the light irradiation, the photocatalysts and pollutants reached to equilibrium by stirring in dark condition for 60 min. Moreover, dark experiment was performed in presence of optimal photocatalyst without any irradiation for 150 min to observe the role of the light irradiation. A LED lamp (50 W) was fixed on the top of the reactor as a visible-light source. Emission spectrum of this source has been reported elsewhere [45]. This source has high intensity in visible range and the intensity rapidly decreases at wavelengths near to UV and IR regions. After the light illumination for every 10 min, the solution was collected to measure the concentration changes of the MB, RhB, fuchsine, and phenol solutions at 664, 553, 540, and 270 nm by the spectrophotometer. The diphenylcarbazide photometric analysis performed for investigation Cr(VI) concentration at 540 nm [46].

The XRD patterns in Fig. 1 demonstrate two typical g-C3N4 peaks at 2θ = 27.5° and 13.5°, which are devoted to the (002) and (100) crystal planes (JCPDS No. 87-1526) [47]. The sharp peak at 27.5° originated from the interlayer reflection of aromatic crystal structure. The weaker peak at 13.5° attributed to the in planar structures of conjugated aromatic systems. For the ag-C3N4-1M, ag-C3N4-2M, ag-C3N4-3M, and ag-C3N4-4M samples, the intensity of diffraction peak at 27.5° diminished, which is the characteristic peak of bulk g-C3N4, denoting formation of layered g-C3N4 structure. Due to the low content of CDs added over the ag-C3N4 sheets, no new characteristic peaks for the ag-C3N4-3M/CDs (0.5 mL) nanocomposite are seen and the same XRD pattern was observed in the resulting nanocomposite [48]. Absence of impurity diffraction peaks clearly displayed high purity of the prepared photocatalysts.

The EDX analysis of the synthesized photocatalysts was also carried out to determine the elements presented in the samples. As expected, only C and N elements were discovered in EDX spectrum regarding g-C3N4 in Fig. 2 (a), which suggests that pure g-C3N4 has obtained. For the EDX spectra of ag-C3N4-3M and ag-C3N4-3M/CDs (0.5 mL) samples, besides C and N elements, the O element is also discovered. The O element, appeared in the spectra, is owing to the presence of different functional groups like hydroxyl, carbonyl, and carboxyl in CDs and also because of the O element presented in H2O2. Therefore, the results revealed that CDs have been dispersed well on the surface of ag-C3N4. Additionally, to further investigate the composition and element distributions in the ag-C3N4-3M/CDs (0.5 mL) nanocomposite, the EDX elemental mapping test was performed. As demonstrated in Fig. 2(b–e), the elemental mappings clearly revealed the uniform spatial distribution and coexistence of the elements, including C, N, and O confirming formation of the binary nanocomposite. In addition to the above mentioned items, the contents of elements in the ag-C3N4-(1–4 M) samples and different nanocomposites of the ag-C3N4-3M/CDs nanocomposite were computed and they are shown in Table 1 .

In order to study the morphology and microstructure of the resultant ag-C3N4-3M/CDs (0.5 mL) nanocomposite, SEM, TEM, and HRTEM images were implemented and the images are observed in Fig. 3 . Significantly, it could be obviously shown in Fig. 3a that nanoparticles of CDs have successfully grown and well adhered to the surface of g-C3N4 in the ag-C3N4-3M/CDs (0.5 mL) photocatalyst. It is also seen from TEM image, that there are black particles and gray areas, which are related to the CDs and g-C3N4 counterparts, respectively (Fig. 3b). Fig. 3c displays typical HRTEM image of the nanocomposite, in which the d-spacing of g-C3N4 was defined to be 0.326 nm, corresponding to the lattice fringe of (002) planes, while the spacing of 0.320 nm was assigned to the (002) plane of CDs [48,49]. Based on the above results, it was indicated that the nanocomposite was indeed formed from two components.

The surface compositions and chemical states of the ag-C3N4-3M/CDs (0.5 mL) catalyst were further examined using XPS. The full spectrum of XPS is presented in Fig. 4 a. It is observed that C, N, and O elements are presented. Additionally, the C 1 s peak at binding energy of 284.6 eV is assigned to the adventitious hydrocarbon from the instrument [50]. In the C 1 s spectrum (Fig. 4b), three peaks at 284.8, 289.4, and 286.0 eV were detected, which can be ascribed to sp2 (CC), CNH2 group of g-C3N4, and COH bonds of CDs particles, respectively [50–52]. In Fig. 4c, two peaks were detected at 398.4 and 400.6 eV in the high-resolution XPS spectrum of N 1 s, which ascribed to CNC and NH groups in g-C3N4, respectively [53]. The O 1 s spectrum (Fig. 4d) could be deconvoluted into three peaks at 531.8, 532.1, and 534.1 eV, that are derived from O-containing groups, COC, and CO moieties of CDs, respectively [35,54,55]. These binding energies verified the successful introduction of CDs into the ag-C3N4-3M photocatalyst.

For further investigation of the structure, the functional groups of the g-C3N4, ag-C3N4-3M, and ag-C3N4-3M/CDs (0.5 mL) samples were demonstrated by FT-IR spectroscopy. The sharp absorption peak at 810 cm−1 is related to the out-of-plane vibration of heptazine ring of g-C3N4 [41,56]. The peaks located in the range of 1200–1700 cm−1 are corresponding to the CN stretching of g-C3N4. The wide absorption band around 3000–3600 cm−1 originates from stretching vibration modes of terminal NH and OH of H2O [57]. As Fig. 5 a shows, due to similar FT-IR spectra of the ag-C3N4-3M and g-C3N4, no major changes in polymeric g-C3N4 structure occured after treating with H2O2. For the ag-C3N4-3M/CDs (0.5 mL) photocatalyst, the peaks in the regions of 1600–1770 and 1350-1460 cm−1 were ascribed to the CO and CH2 groups available in CDs, respectively [58,59].

For further studying the influence of H2O2 treating and the amount of CDs decoration on the optical properties, UV–vis DR spectra of the g-C3N4, ag-C3N4-3M, g-C3N4-3M/CDs (0.25, 0.5, 0.75, and 1 mL), and CDs samples were tested. As observed from Fig. 5b, absorption edge at 460 nm for the pristine g-C3N4 was red shifted to 475 nm in the spectrum of the ag-C3N4-3M sample [33]. After introduction of CDs on the surface of the ag-C3N4-3M photocatalyst, the absorbance was slightly increased in visible area, because of reduction of the band gap.

More importantly, the upconverted PL spectra of the as-synthesized CDs have also been investigated with excitation at long-wavelengths, as manifested in Fig. 6 . Remarkably, the as-synthesized CDs exhibit clear upconversion properties, and give the upconverted PL emissions located at 504 nm and hardly shift when excited by long-wavelength lights (from 500 to 900 nm). The upconverted PL properties of CDs should be attributed to the multiphoton active process, in which the simultaneous absorption of two or more photons leads to the emission of light at a shorter wavelength than the excitation wavelength [60,61].

Fig. 7 displays the TG curves for the samples in the range of 20–700 °C. As seen, the samples have a very low weight loss up to 300 °C, due to the loss of surface adsorbed water. The g-C3N4 began to decompose at ∼500 °C and it was noticeable decomposed at 680 °C. As expected, the decomposition temperature of the ag-C3N4-3M and ag-C3N4-3M/CDs samples are clearly lower than that of the pure g-C3N4, because of the catalytic action of exfoliation and the integrated CD particles. Based on the TG analysis, the photocatalysts were completely decomposed, due to the presence of nonmetallic elements in their structures.

Fig. 8 a displays photocatalytic degradation of RhB over the g-C3N4 and the treated g-C3N4 with different solutions of H2O2 under visible light. Blank test (RhB under photolysis condition) shows that the photolysis of RhB is extremely slow and only 8% of RhB is degraded during 360 min of the light irradiation. As shown in Fig. 8a, the rate of RhB degradation increases by exfoliation of g-C3N4 with H2O2, because of the production of more oxidizing agents. Also, it is worth noting that by increasing the concentration of H2O2, the photocatalytic activity firstly increased and then decreased. Therefore, an appropriate amount of H2O2 is also crucial for achieving the optimum photocatalytic efficiency. Under experimental conditions, the best photocatalytic performance was observed for the g-C3N4 treated with 3 M of H2O2 by complete degradation of RhB in 120 min, which is very shorter than the other photocatalysts. The results of Fig. 8b also verifies excellent ability of the binary nanocomposites in comparison with the g-C3N4 and g-C3N4-3M photocatalysts. As seen, the photocatalytic ability increases when the CDs was integrated with the ag-C3N4-3M. Among the ag-C3N4-3M/CDs photocatalysts, the ag-C3N4-3M/CDs nanocomposite with 0.5 mL of CDs displayed exceptional activity by complete degradation of RhB within 60 min. For comparison, we combined the pure g-C3N4 with CDs to fabricate the g-C3N4/CDs (0.5 mL) nanocomposite and its photocativity was investigated. It is obvious that photocatalytic activity of the ag-C3N4-3M/CDs (0.5 mL) nanocomposite is exceptionally greater than the g-C3N4/CDs (0.5 mL) nanocomposite, which is arising from contribution of CDs and modification of pure g-C3N4 with H2O2. As the dark experiment displays, without the light irradiation, the pollutant is stable in the presence of the ag-C3N4-3M/CDs (0.5 mL) nanocomposite, confirming that decrease of RhB concentration is related to photodegradation reaction rather that the adsorption process.

Figs. 9 (a–c) displays the absorbance changes for RhB on the g-C3N4, ag-C3N4-3M, and ag-C3N4-3M/CDs (0.5 mL) photocatalysts under visible light. As observed, the absorbance of RhB, at 553 nm, diminished with prolonging the irradiation time. Additionally, in the presence of the ag-C3N4-3M/CDs (0.5 mL) photocatalyst, no absorption peak was seen after 60 min, confirming complete decomposition of RhB. In contrast, only 32 and 68% of RhB were degraded by the g-C3N4 and ag-C3N4-3M photocatalysts, respectively. As it is well known, the RhB photodegradation can occur by two competitive mechanisms of attacking oxidative species to the aromatic chromophore ring, leading to the degradation of RhB structure and decrease of the absorption band without a wavelength shift or by a successive de-ethylation from the aromatic rings, causing significant blue wavelength shifts according to the formation of the different de-ethylated intermediates [62–65]. As can be seen, any shifts were not observed during the degradation reactions by the photocatalysts, confirming aromatic-ring opening pathway instead of N-deethylation mechanism [66].

Fig. 10 a shows the calculated rate constants for the degradation process upon the photocatalysts. Under the similar experimental situations, the degradation rate constant of RhB over the ag-C3N4-3M/CDs (0.5 mL) nanocomposite is 675 × 10−4 min−1, which amazingly reaches about 34.7, 2.4, and 11.1 times as premier as those of the pristine g-C3N4, ag-C3N4-3M, and g-C3N4/CDs (0.5 mL) samples, respectively. For understanding the separation efficiency of the photoinduced charges, the PL emission spectra of different photocatalysts were provided, as the spectra are exhibited in Fig. 10b. It can be found that the order of the PL intensity could be listed as follows: g-C3N4 > ag-C3N4-2M > ag-C3N4-4M > ag-C3N4-3M > ag-C3N4-3M/CDs (1 mL) > ag-C3N4-3M/CDs (0.5 mL). As seen, the pure g-C3N4 has the strongest emission, owing to the fastest recombination of e-/h+ pairs. In the ag-C3N4-2M, due to incomplete exfoliation and thus fast recombination of charge carriers, an enhancement in the PL intensity is clearly seen comparing with the ag-C3N4-3M, as seen in Fig. 10b. Interestingly, compared to the ag-C3N4-3M sample, high concentration of H2O2 caused an increase in the PL intensity in the ag-C3N4-4M photocatalyst. This increase can be attributed to blocking some of the active sites of g-C3N4 by H2O2, resulting decrease life time of the charges in the ag-C3N4-4M. Additionally, the ag-C3N4-3M/CDs (0.5 mL) nanocomposite showed remarkably diminished PL intensity, when compared with the other composites. These outcomes suggested that the treatment of g-C3N4 with H2O2 and introduction of CDs on the surface of that can effectively impeded the recombination of photogenerated e-/h+. But, it should be considered that by adding more CDs, an enhancement in the PL intensity is clearly seen, leading to reduction of the photocatalytic activity, as displayed in Fig. 10a. This increase can be attributed to agglomeration of CDs particles, resulting in destruction of the heterojunctions between ag-C3N4 and CDs, leading to decrease life time of the e‒/h+ pairs in the ag-C3N4-3M/CDs (1 mL) nanocomposite in comparison with the ag-C3N4-3M/CDs (0.5 mL) nanocomposite. Hence, separation of the photogenerated charge carriers will be greatly hindered, resulting in decrease of the photocatalytic performance, as observed in Fig. 10a. Similar results have been observed in the literature for different multi-component photocatalysts [67,68].

In the next step, the textural characteristics of the g-C3N4, ag-C3N4-3M, and ag-C3N4-3M/CDs (0.5 mL) photocatalysts were determined using nitrogen adsorption method. The isotherms in Fig. 10c can be classified as type IV with H3 hysteresis loop for all samples, which indicated the presence of mesopore structures. As observed, specific surface area of the ag-C3N4-3M powder (45.1 m2 g−1) is three times larger than that of the pure g-C3N4 (14.6 m2 g−1), indicating high exfoliation of the pristine g-C3N4 during the treatment process (Table 2 ). So, the higher specific surface area of the treated g-C3N4 can simplify adsorption of the dye molecules. Thus, the ag-C3N4-3M sample is expected to possess a better photocatalytic activity than the untreated sample for the degradation of organic pollutants. But, after combining CD particles with the ag-C3N4-3M sample, the surface areas decreased to 19.7 m2 g−1 for the ag-C3N4-3M/CDs (0.5 mL) sample. This behavior could be ascribed to blockage of micropores belonging to the treated g-C3N4 [69]. As seen, the ag-C3N4-3M compared with ag-C3N4-xM (x = 2, 4) and ag-C3N4-3M/CDs (0.5 mL) nanocomposite displayed superior rate constant, which is related to the improved surface area, increased light absorption, and separation of the photoinduced charges as confirmed by BET surface, UV–vis DRS, and PL spectroscopy analyses, respectively.

According to the above outcomes, the degradation reactions of dyes for the boosted photocatalytic ability of the ag-C3N4-3M/CDs nanocomposites take place by multiple charge transfer pathways under visible-light irradiation, as shown in Fig. 11 . When CDs were integrated with the ag-C3N4-3M photocatalyst, the increased photocatalytic ability can be related to the following reasons. At first, by combining CDs with the ag-C3N4-3M, the light absorption capability of the binary photocatalyst was improved, owing to upconversion characteristics, leading to excitation of the photocatalyst to generate more charge carriers [70]. Hence, under visible-light illumination, e–/h+ pairs are generated with the excitation of g-C3N4 by absorbing lights with wavelengths smaller than 500 nm, owing to its medium band gap of 2.7 eV. However, in the case of the ag-C3N4-3M/CDs nanocomposites, the lights with wavelengths larger than 500 nm could be converted to wavelengths smaller than 500 nm by CDs, due to upconversion of long wavelengths to short wavelengths [70,71]. In addition, particles of CDs can act as good electron acceptors; hence they provide the possibility for transfer of the photoexcited electrons from ag-C3N4 to CDs [72,73]. So, the ag-C3N4-3M/CDs nanocomposites could not only boost production of e–/h+ pairs by extending the absorption region into visible region, but also simplify charge transfer and separation at the junction interface and greatly prevent the recombination of photogenerated e–/h+ pairs. Also, the parallel pathway for the degradation reaction is based on the excitation of dye molecules under visible light. When the light falls over the dye molecules, they absorb some of photons and subsequently electrons are excited from the HOMO to LUMO orbitals of these molecules, so they could act as a sensitizer and inject excited electrons to the CB of ag-C3N4-3M and CDs, followed by self-degradation or decomposition reactions [74–76]. Subsequently, the electrons over particles of CDs and the remained electrons over the surface of ag-C3N4-3M may be catched by molecular oxygen, leading to the production of
O2 − species, which react with H+ ions to produce
OH radicals. The valence band (VB) potential of g-C3N4 ( + 1.57 eV) is more negative than the potential of OH−/•OH ( + 2.69 eV) and H2O/
OH ( + 2.72 eV) systems, hence, the formed h+ over that cannot oxidize OH− or H2O to produce
OH species [23,77]. Given that, h+ are the mainly energetic species in the photocatalytic process, therefore residual h+ within the VB of ag-C3N4-3M could cause to open the aromatic ring of the pollutants and oxidize them to some intermediates. Finally, the formed intermediates can be degraded and also mineralized via the active species (
O2 −,
OH, and h+) into small fragments such as CO2, H2O, and other mineralization products [78,79].

To explore the major oxidative species in the photocatalytic degradation reactions, radical-trapping tests were done by adding three chemicals including benzoquinone (BQ,
O2 − scavenger), tert-Butyl alcohol (t-BuOH,
OH scavenger), and ammonium oxalate (AO, h+ scavenger). It is noteworthy that all reaction conditions were the same as those adopted in the photocatalytic activity measurements. Also, the concentration of all scavengers was fixed as 1 mM. As shown in Fig. 12 , the addition of these compounds upon the visible-light illumination considerably reduced the photocatalytic activity. When AO, t-BuOH, and BQ were added, the rate constant was reduced from 675 × 10−4 min-1 to 98.4 × 10−4 min-1, 23.4 × 10-4 min-1, and 15.7 × 10−4 min-1, respectively. Thus,
O2 −,
OH, and h+ species are the major reactive species during the degradation process. Additionally, the degradation rate constant of RhB was considerably decreased by N2 bubbling in the reaction system, confirming the important role of dissolved O2 in scavenging electrons [80,81].

Indeed, stability of a photocatalyst is significant from an economic point of view. As shown in Fig. 13 , the degradation percentages are 100, 95, 88, and 81% for runs 1, 2, 3, and 4, respectively, indicated the good stability of the ag-C3N4-3M/CDs (0.5 mL) photocatalyst in these experiments with small deactivation. Hence, it is reusable in wastewater treatment and this suggests the birth of economically viable photocatalyst for water treatment.

Photocatalytic capabilities of the as-fabricated g-C3N4, ag-C3N4-3M, and ag-C3N4-3M/CDs (0.5 mL) samples were evaluated by measuring their activities towards degradations of four typical organic contaminants (MB, RhB, fuchsine, and phenol) and photoreduction of Cr(VI) upon visible light. As shown in Fig. 14 (a–d), the ag-C3N4-3M/CDs (0.5 mL) photocatalyst displayed the best results in removals of MB, RhB, fuchsine, phenol and Cr(VI). The photocatalytic ability of the binary nanocomposite is 34.7, 14.5, 20.7, 11.6, and 40.9-folds as superior as that of the pristine g-C3N4 for removals of RhB, MB, fuchsine, phenol, and Cr(VI), respectively. As a result, it is suggested that the synthesized binary photocatalyst can be utilized in actual environmental remediation processes with significant activity. As a bright note, rate constants for degradation of the cationic dyes (RhB, MB, and fuchsine) over the g-C3N4, ag-C3N4-3M, and ag-C3N4-3M/CDs (0.5 mL) photocatalysts are higher than that of phenol. Because, the surface charge of g-C3N4 is negative around pH 7 [82], leading to considerable adsorption of cationic dyes over the negatively charges g-C3N4-based photocatalysts. Therefore, the degradation rate constants significantly increased for the dye pollutants rather than phenol.

The TOC contents were measured for aqueous solutions of RhB, MB, fuchsine, and phenol during 60 min of the light irradiation over the ag-C3N4-3M/CDs (0.5 mL) nanocomposite and the results are shown in Fig. 15 . For this purpose, samples of the solution were collected at different time intervals (−60, 0, 30 and 60 min) and TOC values (in ppm) were measured. As can be seen, after 60 min of the light irradiation, the TOC contents of the RhB, MB, fuchsine, and phenol solutions decreased to 21.3, 28.7, 34.4, and 56.4%, respectively. Hence, it was concluded that during the degradation reaction of the pollutants, the mineralization reactions are considerably taken place.

Briefly, we developed a simple method to exceptional enhancing visible-light-induced photocatalytic ability of bulk g-C3N4 by treating with H2O2 and integrating with carbon dots. The binary ag-C3N4/CDs photocatalysts applied for removal of four organic and one inorganic contaminants under visible light. In comparison with the pristine g-C3N4 and ag-C3N4, the binary nanocomposite fabricated by treating with 3 M H2O2 and combining with 0.5 mL of CDs displayed the superior activity. Photocatalytic ability of the ag-C3N4-3M/CDs (0.5 mL) nanocomposite was 14.5, 34.7, 20.7, 11.6, and 40.9 times superior than those of the pristine g-C3N4 for degradations of MB, RhB, fuchsine, phenol and photoreduction of Cr(VI), respectively. The improved performances was ascribed to improved surface area due to exfoliation of bulk g-C3N4, higher light absorption capability due to introduction of H and O elements into the framework of g-C3N4, and facile separation of the e−/h+ pairs. Moreover, the photocatalytic experiments exhibited that
O2 −, h+, and
OH species played dominant roles in the degradation of RhB. Recovery of the nanocomposite was studied and the photoactivity showed slightly reduction after four runs. This work provides some ideas for future design of novel photocatalysts based on ag-C3N4/CDs for efficiently elimination of organic and inorganic pollutants.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors genuinely appreciate the financial support of this research from University of Mohaghegh Ardabili.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jphotochem.2019.02.002.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:
